title: 9/11 attacks destroy the World Trade Center
blurb: Al-Qaeda hijackers crashed two jetliners into the twin towers of the World Trade Center, collapsing both and killing 2,753 people in New York City. It was the deadliest terrorist attack in U.S. history.
detail: Nineteen hijackers seized four planes; two struck the North and South towers in Lower Manhattan while a third hit the Pentagon and a fourth crashed in Pennsylvania. The 110-story towers fell within hours, and the disaster killed 2,977 people from 90 nations across all sites. The attacks reshaped New York's skyline, security posture and rebuilding politics for decades.

title: Bloomberg elected mayor after 9/11
blurb: Media entrepreneur Michael Bloomberg won the mayoralty just weeks after the September 11 attacks, defeating Democrat Mark Green. He took office as the 108th mayor on Jan. 1, 2002.
detail: Running as a Republican with Rudy Giuliani's endorsement, Bloomberg framed himself as a manager who could steer the city's recovery and finances after the attacks. He would serve three terms through 2013, reshaping public health, land use and education policy. His victory came in a city where registered Democrats vastly outnumbered Republicans.

title: Smoke-Free Air Act signed, effective 2003
blurb: Mayor Bloomberg signed a sweeping expansion of the Smoke-Free Air Act, banning smoking in nearly all bars and restaurants. The ban took effect March 30, 2003.
detail: The law extended a 1995 ordinance to cover virtually all indoor workplaces regardless of prior exemptions. It drew intense opposition from bar and restaurant owners but became a national model for indoor smoking bans. Cigarette use in the city fell markedly in the years that followed.

title: Northeast blackout darkens the city
blurb: A cascading grid failure that began in Ohio cut power to New York City and much of the Northeast starting just after 4 p.m. About 55 million people across the U.S. and Canada lost electricity.
detail: A transmission line fault in Ohio triggered one of the largest blackouts in North American history. In New York City, subways stalled and commuters walked home across bridges, but the night passed with relatively little disorder. Full power was restored to the city over the following two days.

title: Staten Island Ferry crash kills 11
blurb: The ferry Andrew J. Barberi slammed at full speed into a concrete pier at the St. George terminal, killing 11 people and injuring dozens. The pilot was found to have been impaired.
detail: The vessel crashed into the maintenance pier around 3:21 p.m. as passengers crowded forward to disembark. Investigators found the assistant captain was impaired by medication and that a two-pilot docking rule had not been enforced. The pilot and the city's ferry director later pleaded guilty to seaman's manslaughter.

title: Freedom Tower cornerstone laid
blurb: A 20-ton granite cornerstone for the Freedom Tower, later One World Trade Center, was laid at Ground Zero. Construction stalled until 2006 amid disputes over money and design.
detail: Gov. George Pataki chose Independence Day for the symbolic ceremony as a statement of resilience after 9/11. Design, security and financing disputes among the Port Authority, developer Larry Silverstein and state officials delayed real construction until major issues were resolved in April 2006. The tower would eventually rise to a symbolic 1,776 feet.

title: Protests grip Republican convention
blurb: As the Republican National Convention met at Madison Square Garden, an estimated 100,000-plus people marched past the arena in one of the largest protests in U.S. history. Hundreds were arrested over the week.
detail: The convention, held Aug. 30 to Sept. 2, renominated President George W. Bush in the city still marked by 9/11. United for Peace and Justice organized the main Aug. 29 march, and mass arrests during the week later led to large legal settlements over police tactics. It was the first Republican convention ever held in New York City.

title: Transit strike halts subways and buses
blurb: Transport Workers Union Local 100 struck the MTA, shutting down all subway and bus service for three days. Millions of commuters were forced to walk, bike or carpool in December cold.
detail: Negotiations over pensions, wages and retirement broke down, and the union walked out at 3 a.m. Dec. 20 in defiance of the Taylor Law barring public-worker strikes. Service resumed after the strike ended Dec. 22, and the union faced heavy fines. The local's executive board accepted a 37-month contract on Dec. 27.

title: City bans artificial trans fats
blurb: The Board of Health voted unanimously to make New York the first U.S. city to ban artificial trans fats in restaurants. Full compliance was required by mid-2008.
detail: The rule barred food served in restaurants from containing more than 0.5 grams of artificial trans fat per serving, targeting frying oils and baked goods. It was part of Bloomberg-era public health initiatives that also included calorie labeling. The federal government did not enact a comparable nationwide ban until 2018.

title: Bloomberg launches PlaNYC
blurb: On Earth Day, the city unveiled PlaNYC, a long-range sustainability plan with 127 initiatives to cut emissions, expand parks and prepare for a million more residents. It became a national model for city climate planning.
detail: PlaNYC set goals for cleaner energy, brownfield cleanup, affordable housing and a 30 percent cut in greenhouse gas emissions. Its most contentious proposal, congestion pricing for Manhattan, stalled in Albany in 2008 but seeded a policy revived a decade later. The plan later evolved into OneNYC under Mayor de Blasio.

title: Lehman Brothers collapse jolts Wall Street
blurb: New York-based Lehman Brothers filed the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history, deepening the global financial crisis. The failure of the fourth-largest investment bank rattled markets worldwide.
detail: Weighed down by subprime mortgage exposure, Lehman collapsed after rescue talks with Bank of America and Barclays fell through and the government declined a bailout. The filing listed more than $600 billion in assets and triggered a financial panic that reshaped Wall Street. New York's finance-dependent economy and tax base were hit hard.

title: Council extends term limits for Bloomberg
blurb: The City Council voted 29-22 to loosen term limits so Mayor Bloomberg could seek a third term. He signed the measure Nov. 3 and won reelection in 2009.
detail: Bloomberg argued that his financial expertise was needed amid the 2008 crisis, but the move to override two voter referendums on term limits drew sharp criticism. He narrowly won a third term in 2009. Voters later restored the two-term limit for city offices.

title: New Yankee Stadium and Citi Field open
blurb: The Yankees and Mets both opened new ballparks in the Bronx and Queens in April 2009. The two stadiums together cost well over $2 billion.
detail: The new Yankee Stadium, replacing the 1923 original, hosted its first regular-season game April 16 against Cleveland. Citi Field, home of the Mets and successor to Shea Stadium, opened its regular season April 13 against San Diego. Both projects relied on substantial public financing through tax-exempt bonds.

title: 'Miracle on the Hudson' water landing
blurb: US Airways Flight 1549 lost both engines to a bird strike after takeoff from LaGuardia and ditched in the Hudson River. All 155 people aboard survived.
detail: Captain Chesley 'Sully' Sullenberger and First Officer Jeffrey Skiles glided the Airbus A320 to a water landing off Midtown less than four minutes after takeoff. Ferries and boats rushed to rescue passengers from the frigid river. The episode became a symbol of professional composure and was later dramatized in a Hollywood film.

title: High Line elevated park opens
blurb: The first section of the High Line, a park built on an abandoned elevated freight rail line, opened on Manhattan's West Side. It became a global model for adaptive reuse and spurred a wave of development.
detail: The initial stretch ran from Gansevoort Street to 20th Street, converting derelict tracks into a landscaped promenade. Later sections extended the park north, and it drew millions of visitors annually. Its success accelerated real estate investment in Chelsea and the surrounding neighborhoods.

title: Failed Times Square car bomb
blurb: A car bomb left in a Nissan SUV in Times Square failed to detonate after vendors spotted smoke and alerted police. Faisal Shahzad was arrested two days later and sentenced to life in prison.
detail: The crude device, packed with propane, gasoline and fireworks, began smoking near the Minskoff Theatre but failed to explode on a crowded Saturday evening. Shahzad, a naturalized U.S. citizen born in Pakistan, was caught at JFK trying to flee to Dubai. He pleaded guilty to terrorism charges and received a life sentence.

title: New York legalizes same-sex marriage
blurb: Gov. Andrew Cuomo signed the Marriage Equality Act, making New York the sixth and most populous state to allow same-sex marriage. The law took effect July 24, 2011.
detail: The bill cleared a Republican-controlled state Senate after intense lobbying, a milestone in the national marriage-equality movement. The first weddings took place at midnight on July 24, and thousands of couples married in the following days. New York's size gave the victory outsize national significance ahead of the 2015 Supreme Court ruling.

title: Hurricane Irene forces first mass evacuation
blurb: Facing Hurricane Irene, Mayor Bloomberg ordered the city's first mandatory evacuation of low-lying areas and shut the entire subway system. Irene weakened before landfall and spared the city major damage.
detail: About 370,000 residents were ordered to leave flood-prone zones as the MTA halted all subway, bus and commuter rail service at noon Aug. 27. Irene came ashore at Coney Island the next morning already weakened to a tropical storm. The dry run in preparedness would be tested far more severely a year later by Sandy.

sources.name: Wikipedia: Effects of Hurricane Irene in New York

title: 9/11 Memorial opens at Ground Zero
blurb: The National September 11 Memorial opened to the public a day after its dedication on the attacks' 10th anniversary. Twin reflecting pools sit in the footprints of the fallen towers.
detail: Designed by Michael Arad, the memorial's pools feature the largest manmade waterfalls in North America and bronze parapets inscribed with the names of the nearly 3,000 victims. The dedication ceremony on Sept. 11, 2011, brought families to the plaza. The accompanying museum would open in 2014.

title: Occupy Wall Street takes Zuccotti Park
blurb: Protesters against economic inequality set up an encampment in Zuccotti Park near Wall Street, popularizing the slogan 'We are the 99%.' Police cleared the camp on Nov. 15.
detail: The occupation began with a march of about 2,000 people and grew into a national and global movement over nearly two months. Demonstrators highlighted wealth inequality and the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis. Around 200 people were arrested when the NYPD evicted the encampment in a pre-dawn operation.

title: Barclays Center opens in Brooklyn
blurb: The Barclays Center arena opened in Brooklyn as the new home of the NBA's Brooklyn Nets, relocated from New Jersey. Part-owner Jay-Z played the first concerts a week later.
detail: The arena anchored the contested Atlantic Yards, later Pacific Park, redevelopment near downtown Brooklyn. It returned major-league sports to the borough for the first time since the Dodgers left in 1957. The venue later also hosted the WNBA's New York Liberty and the NHL's Islanders.

title: Hurricane Sandy devastates the city
blurb: Superstorm Sandy drove a record storm surge into New York City, flooding Lower Manhattan, tunnels and coastal neighborhoods. The storm killed dozens of residents and caused about $19 billion in damage.
detail: A surge near 14 feet inundated 51 square miles, knocked out power to hundreds of thousands and flooded subway tunnels and the Financial District. Roughly 43 to 44 city residents died, many by drowning, and neighborhoods in Staten Island, the Rockaways and Coney Island were hardest hit. Sandy spurred a decade of coastal resilience planning.

title: One World Trade Center reaches full height
blurb: Workers hoisted the final piece of One World Trade Center's spire, bringing the tower to a symbolic 1,776 feet. That November a height panel declared it the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere.
detail: The 1,776-foot pinnacle references the year of the Declaration of Independence. In November 2013 the Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat ruled the spire counted toward the building's official height, settling a debate with Chicago's Willis Tower. The rebuilt tower rose on the site of the destroyed twin towers.

title: Citi Bike launches bike share
blurb: New York launched Citi Bike, then the largest bike-share system in the United States, with 6,000 bikes at more than 300 stations. Riders logged more than 6,000 trips on opening day.
detail: Sponsored by Citigroup and run without direct city subsidy at launch, the system initially covered Manhattan below 59th Street and parts of Brooklyn. It grew over the following decade into a major mode of transportation across multiple boroughs. The program had been delayed in part by damage from Hurricane Sandy.

title: Judge rules stop-and-frisk unconstitutional
blurb: A federal judge ruled in Floyd v. City of New York that the NYPD's stop-and-frisk practices violated the Constitution by unlawfully targeting Black and Latino New Yorkers. The court ordered a monitor to oversee reforms.
detail: Judge Shira Scheindlin found the department's tactics violated the Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments and amounted to racial profiling. The ruling followed years of data showing hundreds of thousands of annual stops concentrated among minority men. The incoming de Blasio administration declined to appeal, and recorded stops fell sharply.

title: de Blasio elected mayor
blurb: Bill de Blasio won the mayoralty in a landslide, becoming the first Democrat to lead New York City since 1989. He campaigned against a 'tale of two cities' of inequality.
detail: De Blasio took more than 73 percent of the vote against Republican Joe Lhota and was sworn in Jan. 1, 2014, in a ceremony officiated by former President Bill Clinton. His agenda centered on universal pre-kindergarten, affordable housing and curbing stop-and-frisk. He would serve two terms through 2021.

title: 9/11 Memorial Museum opens
blurb: The National September 11 Memorial Museum opened to the public beneath the memorial plaza. It was dedicated May 15 with President Obama and past mayors in attendance.
detail: Built largely below grade around the exposed foundations of the twin towers, the museum houses artifacts, recorded testimony and a repository for unidentified remains. Its dedication drew Presidents Obama and Clinton along with former mayors Dinkins, Giuliani and Bloomberg. Tens of thousands of first responders and victims' relatives visited before the public opening.

title: Eric Garner dies in police chokehold
blurb: Eric Garner died after an NYPD officer put him in a banned chokehold during an arrest on Staten Island, gasping 'I can't breathe' repeatedly. A grand jury declined to indict the officer that December.
detail: Officers approached Garner on suspicion of selling loose cigarettes, and bystander video captured his repeated pleas as he was pinned down. The medical examiner ruled the death a homicide. The Dec. 3 grand jury decision not to charge Officer Daniel Pantaleo sparked large protests across the city, and 'I can't breathe' became a rallying cry.

title: One World Trade Center opens
blurb: One World Trade Center opened for business as publisher Conde Nast moved its first employees into the rebuilt tower. It stood as the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere.
detail: Conde Nast, relocating from Times Square, became the anchor tenant on floors 20 through 44. The opening capped 13 years of contentious rebuilding at Ground Zero. The 104-story tower rose to a symbolic 1,776 feet on the northwest corner of the World Trade Center site.

title: Two NYPD officers ambushed in Brooklyn
blurb: Officers Rafael Ramos and Wenjian Liu were shot and killed as they sat in their patrol car in Bedford-Stuyvesant. The gunman, who had cited Eric Garner's death, killed himself in a nearby subway station.
detail: Ismaaiyl Brinsley shot the two officers through the passenger window and then fled into the subway, where he took his own life. He had shot and wounded his ex-girlfriend in Baltimore earlier that day. The killings deepened tensions between the NYPD and City Hall amid the post-Garner protests.

title: Pope Francis visits New York City
blurb: Pope Francis made his only visit to New York City, praying at the 9/11 Memorial, addressing crowds and celebrating Mass at Madison Square Garden. Nearly 20,000 people attended the arena service.
detail: During the Sept. 24-25 stop the pope visited St. Patrick's Cathedral, addressed the United Nations, processed through Central Park and led Mass at the Garden. His visit to the 9/11 Memorial offered an interfaith moment of reflection. The trip drew enormous crowds and heavy security across Manhattan.

title: Chelsea bombing injures 29
blurb: A pressure-cooker bomb exploded on West 23rd Street in Chelsea, injuring 29 people. Ahmad Khan Rahimi was arrested days later and sentenced to life in prison.
detail: The device, packed with shrapnel, detonated on a crowded Saturday evening, and a second unexploded bomb was found nearby. Rahimi, inspired by militant groups, had also planted a device in New Jersey earlier that day. He was captured after a shootout with police in Linden, New Jersey.

title: Second Avenue Subway opens
blurb: The first phase of the Second Avenue Subway opened on the Upper East Side, the city's biggest subway expansion in half a century. New stations at 72nd, 86th and 96th streets extended the Q line.
detail: Nearly a century after it was first proposed, the line finally opened with a ceremonial New Year's Eve ride and regular service the next day. About 48,000 riders used the new stations on opening day. Later phases to extend the line farther into East Harlem remained in planning.

title: Cornell Tech campus opens
blurb: Cornell Tech opened its permanent campus on Roosevelt Island, the product of a Bloomberg-era competition to grow New York's tech economy. The first phase spanned five acres.
detail: The city awarded Cornell and Technion land and $100 million in a 2011 applied-sciences competition aimed at diversifying the economy beyond finance. The initial buildings included an academic center, a startup hub called The Bridge and a residential tower. The campus is planned to expand to about 12 acres.

title: Truck attack on Hudson River bike path
blurb: A man drove a rented truck down a Lower Manhattan bike path, killing eight people and injuring many others. It was the city's deadliest terrorist attack since 9/11.
detail: Sayfullo Saipov steered the truck about a mile along the West Side bike path before crashing into a school bus and being shot by police. Five of the dead were tourists from Argentina and another was from Belgium. Investigators found material pledging allegiance to the Islamic State, and Saipov was later sentenced to life in prison.

title: Amazon scraps HQ2 in Queens
blurb: Amazon canceled plans for a major second headquarters in Long Island City after fierce local opposition. The deal had promised 25,000 jobs and nearly $3 billion in incentives.
detail: Critics objected to the secretive selection process, the size of the subsidies and fears of gentrification, and key state and local politicians voiced opposition. Amazon said it saw no viable path forward and withdrew. The reversal became a flashpoint in debates over corporate incentives and development politics.

title: Hudson Yards opens on the West Side
blurb: The first phase of Hudson Yards, the largest private real estate development in U.S. history, opened over rail yards on Manhattan's West Side. Its centerpiece was the honeycomb-like Vessel structure.
detail: Developed by Related Companies atop a working rail yard, the megaproject added office towers, luxury housing, a mall and public spaces. The 150-foot Vessel, a climbable stairway sculpture, drew crowds but later closed after a series of suicides. The development extended Midtown's commercial core westward.

title: Congestion pricing enacted for Manhattan
blurb: New York's state budget authorized congestion pricing for Manhattan south of 60th Street, the first such program in the United States. Revenue was earmarked for the MTA.
detail: The plan, long championed to reduce traffic and fund transit, cleared Albany after years of failed attempts including a 2008 defeat. Implementation faced federal review and pandemic delays before tolling finally began in 2025. Revenue was legally locked in for MTA capital improvements.

sources.name: Wikipedia: Congestion pricing in New York City

title: Sweeping rent-law overhaul enacted
blurb: New York enacted the Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act, the strongest tenant protections in state history. It made rent regulation permanent and closed longstanding landlord loopholes.
detail: The law repealed vacancy bonuses, capped rent increases tied to building improvements and strengthened protections against overcharges and eviction. It made the rent-stabilization system permanent rather than subject to periodic renewal and allowed it to expand statewide. Landlord groups challenged the changes in court.

title: New York City becomes COVID-19 epicenter
blurb: New York City emerged as the early epicenter of the U.S. coronavirus pandemic, recording its first case Feb. 29 and exploding to hundreds of deaths a day by April. A statewide 'PAUSE' order shut nonessential business March 22.
detail: Hospitals were overwhelmed, refrigerated trucks served as temporary morgues and daily life ground to a halt as case counts soared. Deaths peaked at a weekly average of more than 560 a day in early April. The pandemic reshaped work, transit, housing and the city's economy for years.

title: George Floyd protests sweep the city
blurb: Mass protests over the police killing of George Floyd spread across all five boroughs, prompting the city's first curfew since 1943. Marches were mostly peaceful, though some areas saw looting and clashes.
detail: Demonstrations began May 28 and continued for weeks, with a curfew imposed from June 1 to 7 after property damage in parts of Manhattan. Police made more than a thousand arrests, and a later city investigation criticized NYPD tactics such as kettling. The protests fueled debates over policing and a subsequent city budget fight.

title: Hurricane Ida floods basement apartments
blurb: The remnants of Hurricane Ida dumped record rainfall on New York City in a single hour, overwhelming the sewer system and flooding streets and subways. Thirteen people died, most drowning in basement apartments.
detail: More than three inches of rain fell in one hour, shattering the city's record and swamping low-lying and inland areas outside the floodplain. Most victims drowned in illegal or unregulated basement dwellings, and most were Asian and foreign-born. The disaster spurred new attention to extreme rainfall and basement housing safety.

title: Adams elected mayor
blurb: Former police captain and Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams won the mayoralty, becoming the city's second Black mayor. He took office Jan. 1, 2022.
detail: Adams won a crowded Democratic primary decided by ranked-choice voting and then defeated Republican Curtis Sliwa. He ran on a public-safety message as the city emerged from the pandemic. His single term would be consumed late by a federal corruption case and a decision not to seek the Democratic nomination in 2025.

title: Migrant arrivals strain city shelters
blurb: Starting in spring 2022, tens of thousands of asylum seekers arrived in New York City, some bused from Texas by Gov. Greg Abbott. The influx overwhelmed the shelter system and became a defining crisis of the Adams years.
detail: More than 200,000 migrants passed through city intake in the following years, straining a right-to-shelter system and city budgets. The Adams administration opened emergency shelters, tent sites and hotels and repeatedly pressed Washington for aid. The response reshaped city politics and fiscal debates through 2024.

title: Trump convicted in Manhattan hush-money trial
blurb: A Manhattan jury found Donald Trump guilty on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, making him the first former U.S. president convicted of a crime. The verdict came during his 2024 campaign.
detail: Prosecutors said Trump falsified records to conceal a hush-money payment to Stormy Daniels before the 2016 election. Jurors deliberated nearly 12 hours after a trial that ran from April 15 to May 30. Trump was sentenced in January 2025 to an unconditional discharge, days before his return to the presidency.

title: Mayor Adams indicted on federal charges
blurb: Eric Adams became the first sitting New York City mayor to be federally indicted, charged with bribery and campaign finance offenses. He pleaded not guilty and refused to resign.
detail: The five-count indictment alleged Adams accepted illegal gifts and straw campaign donations tied to Turkish interests, including hotel and travel benefits. The case triggered turmoil at City Hall and among federal prosecutors before it was later dismissed. It contributed to Adams's decision not to seek the 2025 Democratic nomination.

title: Congestion pricing tolls begin
blurb: New York became the first U.S. city to charge a congestion toll, with drivers paying to enter Manhattan below 60th Street. The base daytime car toll started at $9.
detail: The long-delayed Central Business District Tolling Program finally took effect after federal approval, litigation and a temporary pause. Early data showed traffic in the zone down about 11 percent and increased transit ridership. Revenue was directed to MTA capital projects.

sources.name: Wikipedia: Congestion pricing in New York City

title: Mamdani wins Democratic mayoral primary
blurb: State Assemblyman Zohran Mamdani scored an upset victory in the Democratic mayoral primary, defeating former Gov. Andrew Cuomo. Ranked-choice results gave him about 56 percent.
detail: Mamdani, a democratic socialist, ran on affordability themes and out-organized a better-funded Cuomo campaign. Cuomo conceded on primary night in a stunning result. Mamdani went on to win the November general election to become the city's first Muslim and South Asian mayor.

title: Mamdani elected mayor
blurb: Zohran Mamdani won the mayoralty at age 34, becoming New York City's first Muslim and first South Asian mayor. He defeated Andrew Cuomo, running as an independent, and Republican Curtis Sliwa.
detail: Mamdani took about 51 percent of the vote in a race that drew the highest mayoral turnout since 1969, powered by young voters. He was the youngest person elected mayor in generations. He succeeded Eric Adams and took office Jan. 1, 2026.
